talk_title: Multimodal Treatment Strategies and Spatial Niche in Gastric Cancer Peritoneal Metastasis

speaker.name: Sun Young Ra
speaker.affiliation: Yonsei Cancer Center, Department of Medical Oncology, South Korea

biomarkers.name: HER2 status
biomarkers.finding: HER2‑positive patients had ~15% peritoneal involvement versus ~75% in HER2‑negative

biomarkers.name: CMS-like genomic stable subtype
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in peritoneal metastases with EMT and stem‑cell signatures

models.name: Stage IV gastric cancer real‑world cohort
Primary tumor and peritoneal metastasis paired samples
Yonsei Cancer Center Translation Research Platform (Xenium 5K)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 9 paired primary‑peritoneal cases, HER2‑negative, polycohesive histology

clinical_data.n: n=3,200 stage IV gastric cancer patients
clinical_data.population: overall stage IV gastric cancer cohort

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics of matched primary and peritoneal gastric cancer lesions revealed a fibroblast‑rich, dense extracellular matrix niche that physically shields tumor cells from immune effector cells and limits drug delivery. FGFR2 amplification and collagen/ECM‑related pathways are up‑regulated in peritoneal deposits, while immune‑checkpoint activation is reduced, suggesting that CAF‑mediated barrier formation drives immune evasion and therapeutic resistance in gastric cancer peritoneal metastasis.
key_findings: Peritoneal metastasis occurred in 70.8% of stage IV gastric cancer patients (n=3,200) and was associated with markedly poorer overall survival compared with lymph‑node‑only disease.
HER2‑negative gastric cancers showed a higher peritoneal metastasis rate (≈75%) than HER2‑positive cases (≈50%).
FGFR2 amplification was significantly enriched in peritoneal metastasis tissues versus primary tumors, suggesting a potential therapeutic target.
Spatial profiling identified four CAF subtypes (remodeling, immune‑controlling, mesothelial, stress‑related) with remodeling and immune‑controlling CAFs markedly increased in peritoneal lesions.

Peritoneal lesions exhibited higher fibroblast, endothelial, macrophage, and T‑cell infiltration but reduced T‑cell activation and granzyme expression, indicating an immunosuppressive microenvironment.
Antigen‑presentation genes (e.g., cloudin) were down‑regulated in peritoneal metastases, implying reduced efficacy of cloudin‑targeted ADCs in this compartment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dense CAF‑derived extracellular matrix physically blocks immune cell contact and drug penetration, contributing to resistance.
Down‑regulation of cloudin and other ADC targets in peritoneal lesions suggests loss of target expression limits efficacy of ADCs.
open_questions: Can CAF‑targeting agents or matrix‑modulating therapies improve drug delivery and immune infiltration in gastric peritoneal metastasis?
What is the optimal timing and modality (systemic vs intraperitoneal) for delivering FGFR2‑directed therapies?

talk_title: Molecular Heterogeneity in Colorectal Peritoneal Metastasis

speaker.name: Raghav Sundar
speaker.affiliation: Yale University, Department of Medicine, GI Oncology

biomarkers.name: Consensus Molecular Subtype 4 (CMS4)
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in peritoneal metastases; associated with poorer overall survival versus CMS1‑3

biomarkers.name: Moesin expression
biomarkers.finding: Up‑regulated in peritoneal lesions; targeting moesin reduced tumor burden in preclinical models

biomarkers.name: PAI‑1 (SERPINE1)
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in CMS4 peritoneal metastases; anti‑PAI‑1 antibody showed activity in mouse peritoneal models

models.name: CMS‑classified colorectal cancer cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: CMS4 vs CMS1‑3, tested with Mitomycin C and Oxaliplatin

models.name: Moesin and PAI‑1 preclinical mouse models
models.genotype_or_key_features: Peritoneal tumor implantation, therapeutic antibody testing

clinical_data.metric: Overall survival difference by CMS subtype
clinical_data.population: CMS4 vs non‑CMS4 colorectal peritoneal metastasis

clinical_data.metric: Response of CMS4 cell lines to HIPEC agents
clinical_data.value: Mitomycin C IC50 2‑fold lower than Oxaliplatin

MOA_summary: Colorectal peritoneal metastases are highly enriched for the mesenchymal CMS4 subtype, characterized by TGF‑β activation, stromal infiltration, and EMT. Multi‑omic profiling shows that CMS4 tumors retain the same driver mutations as primaries but undergo transcriptional reprogramming and distinct microenvironmental remodeling, leading to resistance to standard HIPEC agents. Targeting stromal components such as moesin or PAI‑1 may overcome this resistance.
key_findings: CMS4 is significantly over‑represented in colorectal peritoneal metastases compared with primary tumors and liver metastases.
Within CMS4, three molecular subgroups (RAS‑mutated mucinous, inflamed, and others) exhibit distinct RAS/TP53 mutation patterns and microenvironment compositions.
Genomic landscapes (TP53, APC, KRAS) are largely conserved between primary and peritoneal lesions, indicating polyclonal seeding rather than subclonal divergence.
CMS4 peritoneal metastases display poor prognosis (HR 0.56 vs non‑CMS4) and are less responsive to oxaliplatin‑based HIPEC, but show greater sensitivity to mitomycin C in vitro.
Targeting stromal proteins moesin or PAI‑1 with monoclonal antibodies reduced tumor burden in preclinical peritoneal models, suggesting a novel therapeutic avenue.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Oxaliplatin‑based HIPEC shows limited efficacy in CMS4 peritoneal metastases, likely due to stromal barrier and drug penetration issues.
Poly‑clonal seeding leads to heterogeneous therapeutic vulnerabilities, reducing the effectiveness of single‑target agents.

combination_strategies.rationale: CMS4 tumors are more sensitive to mitomycin C than oxaliplatin in vitro
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved cytotoxicity in CMS4 cell lines (2‑fold lower IC50)

combination_strategies.rationale: PAI‑1 is up‑regulated in CMS4 peritoneal lesions and drives stromal remodeling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced tumor burden in mouse peritoneal model

open_questions: Can CMS4 classification be reliably performed on limited peritoneal biopsy material to guide therapy?
Will combining stromal‑targeted agents with systemic chemotherapy improve overall survival in CMS4 patients?

talk_title: Mutational Signatures and Cellular Phenotypes in Ovarian Peritoneal Metastasis

speaker.name: Ignacio Garcia
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: HR deficiency (HRD‑DUP/HRD‑DEL signatures)
biomarkers.finding: HRD tumors show higher type‑I interferon and JAK‑STAT signaling and more HLA loss

biomarkers.name: 6p LOH (HLA loss)
biomarkers.finding: Clonal 6p loss enriched in BRCA1‑like HRD‑DUP tumors, associated with T‑cell dysfunction

biomarkers.name: PD‑1/PD‑L1 proximity interactions
biomarkers.finding: Higher PD‑1/PD‑L1 contacts in HRD‑DUP tumors versus HR‑proficient (FBI) tumors

models.name: High‑grade serous ovarian cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈41 patients, primary and peritoneal metastasis samples, ascites

models.name: scRNA‑seq of tumor and immune cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: Cell‑type clusters linked to HR status and anatomical site

models.name: scWGS (single‑cell whole‑genome sequencing)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Hundreds of thousands of cells across 40+ patients, ploidy and chromosome missegregation profiling

models.name: Multiplex IF for cGAS‑positive micronuclei
models.genotype_or_key_features: Quantifies cytosolic DNA in genome‑doubled vs diploid cells

clinical_data.value: HR‑deficient patients have better OS than HR‑proficient

MOA_summary: Whole‑genome signatures separate HR‑deficient (BRCA1/2‑like) from HR‑proficient ovarian tumors. HR‑deficient peritoneal metastases display heightened interferon/JAK‑STAT signaling, increased antigen presentation, and frequent clonal loss of HLA (6p LOH), leading to T‑cell dysfunction. Genome doubling is universal, generating ongoing chromosome missegregation and micronuclei; however, genome‑doubled cells down‑regulate cGAS‑STING signaling to tolerate instability, shaping immune evasion in the peritoneal niche.
key_findings: ≈50% of high‑grade serous ovarian cancers are HR‑deficient, identifiable by tandem‑duplication (BRCA1‑like) or interstitial deletion (BRCA2‑like) signatures.
HR‑deficient tumors up‑regulate type‑I interferon and JAK‑STAT pathways and show higher expression of antigen‑presentation machinery compared with HR‑proficient (FBI) tumors.
Clonal 6p LOH (HLA loss) is enriched in BRCA1‑like HRD‑DUP tumors and correlates with increased T‑cell dysfunction.
All tumors contain genome‑doubled cells; genome doubling drives extensive cell‑specific aneuploidies and micronucleus formation.
cGAS‑positive micronuclei are abundant in diploid cells but inversely correlated with cGAS‑STING signaling in genome‑doubled cells, suggesting adaptive suppression of innate immunity.
Multiplex IF reveals significantly more PD‑1/PD‑L1 proximal interactions in HRD‑DUP tumors, indicating a distinct immune checkpoint landscape.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Clonal HLA loss (6p LOH) provides a mechanism of immune escape in HR‑deficient peritoneal lesions.
Genome doubling suppresses cGAS‑STING signaling, allowing cells with high chromosomal instability to survive.

combination_strategies.rationale: HR‑proficient (FBI) tumors exhibit immunosuppressive TGF‑beta programs
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential re‑activation of anti‑tumor immunity in HR‑proficient peritoneal metastases

combination_strategies.rationale: Increased PD‑1/PD‑L1 interactions in HRD‑DUP tumors suggest checkpoint dependence
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced T‑cell activation in preclinical models

open_questions: What determines the selective suppression of cGAS‑STING signaling after genome doubling?
Can HLA loss be reversed or compensated to restore immunogenicity in HR‑deficient lesions?
